skill.name: ai-video-generation
skill.description: Route AI video requests to the appropriate RunComfy model and provide ready-to-run CLI commands for text-to-video, image-to-video, and video extension workflows.
skill.summary: Generate and extend AI video clips through RunComfy model routes and CLI examples.
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but manual review found they are mostly Markdown examples, links, and model text. The real risks are expected for this skill: running the RunComfy CLI, sending prompts and media URLs to RunComfy, downloading outputs, and storing an API token. No malicious intent, prompt-injection attempt, hidden exfiltration, or arbitrary shell command pattern was found.

medium_findings.title: RunComfy CLI Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill declares Bash access for runcomfy commands and provides npm, npx, login, and runcomfy examples. This is expected for the skill purpose, but it executes an external CLI and should require user authorization and trusted inputs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The declared allowed tool and command blocks clearly instruct the agent to run runcomfy. The commands align with the skill purpose and do not show arbitrary shell execution.

medium_findings.title: Outbound RunComfy API and Asset Network Access
medium_findings.description: The skill sends generation requests to RunComfy, polls status, and downloads generated media. This is legitimate for video generation, but prompts and media URLs leave the local environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly states that the CLI posts to the RunComfy Model API and downloads result URLs. The endpoints are disclosed and scoped to RunComfy domains.

medium_findings.title: API Token Stored in User Config Directory
medium_findings.description: The skill documents that runcomfy login writes an API token to a hidden config path. The file mode is documented as 0600 and CI can use an environment variable, so this is a manageable secret-storage concern.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token path and permissions are explicitly documented on one line. This confirms filesystem secret storage, but the text also includes standard mitigation guidance.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Reference Asset Injection Risk
medium_findings.description: The skill accepts image, audio, and video URLs that can influence generation results. It correctly warns that user-provided reference assets are untrusted and should be treated as task-scoped inputs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risk is semantic rather than a direct code flaw, and the skill includes mitigations. Reference media can still steer generation output unexpectedly.

low_findings.title: Static Blocker Patterns Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby backtick execution and weak cryptography blockers were triggered by Markdown inline code, model identifiers, and prose. No Ruby code, cryptographic implementation, malware logic, or prompt-injection attempt was found in the reviewed skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Static patterns were present, but source context shows Markdown examples or documentation rather than hidden execution logic. No executable Ruby or weak crypto operation appears in the cited context.

dangerous_patterns.title: RunComfy CLI Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill declares Bash access for runcomfy commands and provides npm, npx, login, and runcomfy examples. This is expected for the skill purpose, but it executes an external CLI and should require user authorization and trusted inputs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The declared allowed tool and command blocks clearly instruct the agent to run runcomfy. The commands align with the skill purpose and do not show arbitrary shell execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Outbound RunComfy API and Asset Network Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill sends generation requests to RunComfy, polls status, and downloads generated media. This is legitimate for video generation, but prompts and media URLs leave the local environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly states that the CLI posts to the RunComfy Model API and downloads result URLs. The endpoints are disclosed and scoped to RunComfy domains.

dangerous_patterns.title: API Token Stored in User Config Directory
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents that runcomfy login writes an API token to a hidden config path. The file mode is documented as 0600 and CI can use an environment variable, so this is a manageable secret-storage concern.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The token path and permissions are explicitly documented on one line. This confirms filesystem secret storage, but the text also includes standard mitigation guidance.

content.value_statement: AI video models have different strengths, inputs, and output tradeoffs. This skill routes each request to a suitable RunComfy model and provides focused CLI instructions.
actual_capabilities: Selects a RunComfy route for text-to-video, image-to-video, and video extension requests.
Provides documented runcomfy CLI examples for HappyHorse, Wan, Seedance, Veo, Kling, Hailuo, and Dreamina models.
Explains when to choose models for audio, lip sync, 4K output, character consistency, speed, or physics-aware motion.
Shows required input fields such as prompts, image URLs, audio URLs, duration, aspect ratio, and output directory.
Documents RunComfy token handling, outbound endpoints, download behavior, and common CLI exit codes.
limitations: Requires the RunComfy CLI, RunComfy account access, and a valid token before generation can run.
Sends prompts, media URLs, and generated outputs through RunComfy network services.
Does not validate model availability, pricing, or API schema changes beyond the included documentation.
Generated video quality depends on source media, prompt clarity, model behavior, and RunComfy service availability.

use_cases.title: Social Video Production
use_cases.description: Create short vertical clips from prompts or still images with model guidance for duration, aspect ratio, and audio.

use_cases.title: Product Motion Concepts
use_cases.description: Animate product images, produce controlled rotations, and compare model choices for realistic product movement.

use_cases.title: Cinematic Prototype Shots
use_cases.description: Plan AI-generated ad frames, character beats, and extended video sequences using model-specific prompting patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Text-to-Video Clip
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a short video from a written prompt.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an 8-second 16:9 video of [subject] doing [action] in [setting]. Include natural ambient audio.

prompt_templates.title: Animate a Still Image
prompt_templates.scenario: Use image-to-video with a provided image URL.
prompt_templates.prompt: Animate this image: [image URL]. Keep the subject identity stable. Add [motion], [camera behavior], and [audio description].

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Model for Production Constraints
prompt_templates.scenario: Compare quality, speed, audio, and cost requirements before running a job.
prompt_templates.prompt: I need a video for [goal]. Constraints: [duration], [aspect ratio], [audio needs], [quality tier], [budget or speed]. Pick the best RunComfy route.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Multi-Reference Cinematic Shot
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced planning with reference images, audio, or videos.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a cinematic clip using [reference image roles], [reference video roles], and [audio role]. Preserve [identity or style] and use [camera language].

output_examples.input: Generate a vertical social clip from a portrait image with wind, a smile, and gentle audio.
output: Recommended route: HappyHorse 1.0 image-to-video for identity preservation and in-pass audio.
Expected guidance: use the supplied image URL, describe only the desired motion, set a vertical aspect ratio, and save outputs locally.

output_examples.input: Make a product bottle rotate exactly 180 degrees with no other motion.
output: Recommended route: Veo 3-1 image-to-video for physics-aware object motion.
Expected guidance: anchor the camera, specify the rotation amount, and state that all other elements should remain still.

output_examples.input: Create a talking-head clip that matches a voiceover file.
output: Recommended route: Wan 2-7 text-to-video when a specific audio file must drive lip motion.
Expected guidance: provide the voiceover URL, describe the speaker and scene, and avoid duplicating the transcript in the prompt.

best_practices: Use verified RunComfy CLI installation paths and confirm authentication before starting a generation job.
Provide only task-specific media URLs, and treat third-party reference assets as untrusted inputs.
Choose the model based on the main constraint: audio, lip sync, speed, 4K quality, physics, or character consistency.
anti_patterns: Do not pipe unknown remote install scripts into a shell for setup.
Do not paste API tokens into prompts, logs, tickets, or generated documentation.
Do not use expensive final-quality models for early prompt exploration when a faster route is sufficient.

faq.question: Does this skill generate video locally?
faq.answer: No. It uses the RunComfy CLI to submit jobs to RunComfy services and download generated outputs.

faq.question: Which AI assistants can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: What credentials are required?
faq.answer: RunComfy authentication is required. The skill documents login storage and the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN option for CI environments.

faq.question: Can it create videos from images?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes image-to-video routes for HappyHorse, Veo, Kling, Hailuo, Dreamina, and Seedance models.

faq.question: Can it handle voiceover lip sync?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends Wan 2-7 when a provided audio URL should drive the speaker mouth movement.

faq.question: What should users avoid sharing?
faq.answer: Users should avoid sharing secrets, private media URLs, and reference assets that are not needed for the current task.
